prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call the fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn itself unto a gentle coolness then doth my spirit rise up within me with exceeding gladness

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and their leaves are transformed from the green of summer into raiment of gold and crimson and burnished copper such as no craftsman could fashion with his hands

2:4 And the morning doth come forth with a mist that lieth low upon the meadows and the valleys even as a soft veil spread upon the face of the earth by unseen hands

2:5 The harvest is gathered in from the fields and the orchards do yield up their abundance of apples and pears and the scent thereof is carried upon the wind unto all who walk among them

2:6 Yea the pumpkins do sit upon the earth like great orbs of orange and the corn doth stand in sheaves and the barns are filled with the bounty of the land

2:7 And in the evening the air doth carry the sweet smell of wood smoke from the hearths of homes and families do gather themselves together in warmth and fellowship

2:8 The geese fly southward in their great formations across the sky calling one unto another as they journey and the squirrels do labor with great diligence to store up provision against the winter that is to come

2:9 The days grow shorter and the light of the sun doth take upon itself a quality of gold that is deeper and richer than at any other time of the year

2:10 And when the evening cometh early one may wrap oneself in a warm garment and sit in quietness and watch the stars appear one by one in the heavens which grow ever clearer as the season deepens

2:11 This is the season that speaketh unto my soul of both abundance and surrender for the earth doth give forth its fullness even as it prepareth itself for rest

2:12 Therefore I say unto thee that autumn is the season most dear unto mine heart for in it there is beauty and peace and a gentle reminder that all things have their appointed time under the heavens
